text: being reminded of that wonderful promise that God gives to us there in the book of 2 Corinthians when he says, my grace is sufficient for you, for thee, and so I'm so thankful for that truth. Psalm 23 this evening as we continue with this series, the names of God, and we've looked at a number of them, many of them in fact as we started the series we spent one week and dealt with the name of God, looked at many references, and then we just continued to move on to the next one, and we've had now for a number of weeks the opportunity to deal with Jehovah Ra, the Lord my shepherd, and we've looked at various aspects. We started out by speaking about David and how he so easily would have been able to relate to what was being communicated when God says that he's our shepherd, and I'm thankful this evening that we have the truth that God has provided to us that he indeed is our shepherd. We know that throughout the Bible there are many illustrations, analogies that God uses throughout the scriptures, and he refers to his people as being sheep, and he is the shepherd, and we are the sheep, and he talks about he's the potter, we're the clay, I mean there's many different examples that the Lord uses to help drive home some points for us to understand. Of course we understand the greatest is that he's our father and we're his children, and what a blessing we consider 1 John 3, behold what manner of love the father hath bestowed upon us that we should be called the sons of God, and what a tremendous privilege that is to know the Lord Jesus Christ as our personal savior. We've often said the Lord is the shepherd, he's the good shepherd, great shepherd, chief shepherd, if we were to look at the New Testament and look at those titles given to the Lord Jesus Christ, but of course as we look at Psalm 23, the significance is when David says the Lord is my shepherd, and how significant that is, as we look at this text of scripture we've spent the last couple of Wednesday evenings dealing with the fact that we understand the provision of my shepherd, we understand the protection of my shepherd, and if I were to title the message this evening I would say the presence of my shepherd, the presence of my shepherd. Notice in verse number four, we touched on verse four last Wednesday evening, but the Bible says this, yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, then watch those words, for thou art with me. Thou art with me. Those are four simple words, but how impactful those four words are that the psalmist says thou art with me. I will fear no evil, why, thou art with me, thy rod, thy staff, they comfort me. Sheep by nature are anxious. We understand that the presence of their shepherd, if we speak about the livestock and the literal sheep and the shepherd, they're anxious critters, and yet the presence of their shepherd provides them with a level of security, it provides them with a measure of calmness. This evening as we think about this truth for us as believers, the presence of my shepherd, I want you to notice that as we look at Psalm 23, notice the confidence that David has. Notice that David says thou art with me. David is confident in his understanding of the presence of his shepherd. I want you to notice that in verse number four when David is speaking about this experience he says, yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, he does not ask his shepherd will you be with me. He's confident in the presence of his shepherd. We find that he doesn't ask the question, he doesn't, have you ever been in a situation where maybe a child would ask of a parent, well will you go with me, they find a measure of security in your presence. Maybe there's a procedure that needs to happen medically, maybe there's a dental appointment, I remember going to the dentist for the first time and I remember, I don't like going to the dentist the last time I went to the dentist, amen, but the reality is that I remember the first time and having that feeling of, will my mom or my dad be with me, will they be there in the room, why? Just because their presence provides some level of comfort, some level of security, David doesn't ask the Lord his shepherd, will you be with me in this moment, no, he says I will fear no evil, why? For thou art with me, he had this confidence that his shepherd would be with him. Now remember as we begin this study of this name of God and we spoke about the fact that David was the human penman that God chose to use to give to us this name of God, that we understand that David as a shepherd, he would have understood his place of being with the sheep, he would have understood what happened to his flock when he wasn't there, he would understand that and so we find that he says thou art with me. In the most difficult times of your life and the most difficult times of my life, we can rest assured, we can have the confidence thou art with me, thou art with me. The Lord wants us to have that same level of confidence, the Lord wants us to know that his presence is real, he wants us to know, we understand that the Bible teaches us about some of the attributes of God that he is all knowing, he's omniscient, that he's all powerful, that he's omnipotent, that he's everywhere present, that he's omnipresent. We talk about him being immutable and all of those things, but I want you to understand that his presence is real in our lives and he wants us to have that confidence, he wants us to know, look if you will at Hebrews chapter 13, Hebrews 13 is a familiar passage of scriptures, we deal with the subject matter of the presence of our shepherd and we talk about this promise often, but we look in Hebrews chapter 13 and we find that in verses 5 and 6, God provides for us great truth desiring that we as his people would have this confidence that David had, yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, why? For thou art with me. We find that in here in Hebrews 13 and verse 5, let your conversation be without covetousness and be content with such things as ye have. Then notice, for he hath said, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee, so that we may boldly say, the Lord is my helper and I will not fear what man shall do unto me. It's a passage, a couple of verses that we're familiar with, but why is it repeated here? Because God wants us as his people to have that measure of confidence that David had, he wants us to know that he will never leave us nor forsake us, isn't that a precious truth of God's word? We learn to live daily in the presence of God, we learn to allow this biblical truth, the Lord my shepherd and yes, he provides for me, yes he protects me, but his presence is with me and it should impact each of us, it should have a deep impact in our lives. I believe that David desired for his son Solomon to understand this principle, I believe that David had a desire that it would have an impact in his life, in Solomon's life, the same way that it had had in his life, David's life, and in fact, you don't need to turn there, but in 1 Chronicles 28 20, in the middle of the conversation, David says to Solomon, be strong and of good courage and do it, fear not nor be dismayed for the Lord God, even my God, will be with thee, he will not fail thee nor forsake thee until thou hast finished all the work for the service of the house of the Lord. David had understood this principle, he had this confidence in his own life, but he wants Solomon to understand this as well and he wants it to impact his life. The presence of our shepherd, the presence of my shepherd ought to have impact in my life. You know, it's interesting to understand this reality in life and in our lives, the presence of certain people affects behavior. Is that not true? How many of you use Waze, when you drive around for your maps, you use Waze? You're not going to admit it, but how many of you use it because it tells you where the police are? You understand what I'm saying? And what happens is the presence of a police officer affects the behavior of the driver. How many of you have seen it on the roads, right? You're driving, you're driving, you're driving and you see this guy driving like crazy and all of a sudden there's a police car sitting up there and what has happened, man, he's on his brakes, he's moving over into a different lane and his whole behavior of driving is affected simply by the presence of a police officer. They have a vehicle sitting over on Random Hills Road right now, it's been sitting there for a few days and there's no officer in it. And it cracks me up because I'll be driving on Random Hills Road and some guy, it's only 35, somebody come flying past me and then all of a sudden they'll see that car and you see the brake lights and you just laugh because there's no one in it. But here's what I'd like to see them do. I'm on a rabbit trail right now and I'm going to get back to it. Here's what I'd like to see them do, let it sit there empty for a few days and then put an officer in it. You know why? Because those guys that go flying by and then slow down, they say, oh man, there's no one in there. So, then tomorrow when they drive it, they're going to not slow down. Anyway, the impact of the presence of an individual. It's a simple illustration, but we can all relate to it. But this evening, I want to ask, and I want to begin to make application in our lives is how much has the presence of our shepherd impacted our lives? How does it impact you today? When you understand that the Lord, my shepherd, that He's with me, did it occur to you today? When you went throughout your day, did it occur to you at any moment today that the Lord is with me? Because the Lord, my shepherd, He's with us. He says, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee. I want you to consider this evening just a few examples biblically of those that were impacted by this great truth. Come back to the book of Deuteronomy, if you will. And I first want to start with the impact on Joshua. I want to deal with the impact on Joshua. Look here in Deuteronomy chapter 31, and then look over at Joshua chapter number 1. And I want you to see how this would have a profound impact, this truth that the Lord would be with Him. This truth that we're looking at from Psalm 23, the Lord, my shepherd. We find that here in Deuteronomy 31, Moses, the Bible says, And Moses went and spake these words unto all Israel. And he said unto them, I am an hundred and twenty years old this day. I can no more go out and come in. Also the Lord hath said unto me, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan. The Lord thy God, He will go over before thee. He will destroy these nations before thee, and thou shalt possess them. And Joshua, he shall go over before thee, as the Lord hath said. And the Lord shall do unto them, as He said to Sihon and to Og, king of the Amorites, and unto the land of them whom He destroyed. And the Lord shall give them up before your face, that ye may do unto them according unto all the commandments which I commanded you. Be strong and of a good courage, fear not, nor be afraid of them. For the Lord thy God, He it is that doth go with thee. He will not fail thee, nor forsake thee. Notice in verse 7, And Moses called unto Joshua and said unto him in the sight of all Israel, Be strong and of a good courage, for thou must go with this people unto the land which the Lord hath sworn unto their fathers to give them, and thou shalt cause them to inherit it. And the Lord, He it is that doth go before thee. He will be with thee. He will not fail thee, neither forsake thee. Fear not, neither be dismayed. Moses delivers the message in the first six verses of Deuteronomy 31 to the children of Israel. The Bible says that He said unto them, He spake these words in verse number 1, unto all Israel. He had gathered them. He's giving them this charge. But then in verse number 7 specifically, the Bible says that He calls Joshua, Joshua's gonna be the human leader of the nation of Israel to take this mantle, if you will, from Moses and lead the people of God into the land that God had promised to them. And how impactful these words must have been to Joshua when he hears as Moses is giving him this charge that the Lord, Jehovah God, He it is that doth go before thee. He will be with thee. They knew that there would be some things that they would face when they went into the land. The presence of God makes all the difference. In Joshua chapter number 1, notice if you will now, as we consider the impact on Joshua, that now it's not Moses that's delivering this charge to Joshua. Now it is God Himself that is speaking to Joshua. And God tells us that Moses died and the Lord's going to now speak to Joshua here. And the Bible tells us in verse number 5, as we look at this, the content of what's going on, He says, there shall not any man be able to stand before thee all the days of thy life. Notice what He says, as I was with Moses, so I will be with thee. I will not fail thee nor forsake thee. You think that was encouraging to hear? You think it was impactful? I mean, Joshua was Moses' minister, was he not? Joshua saw, understood what it was when Moses was up on the mountain, all that had taken place. And when God says, I was with Moses, I think Joshua understood, he saw, he was able to easily relate to what that was. And now God is saying, as I was with Him, I will be with thee. I think about looking at the book of Acts and beginning to understand some of the difficulty that the disciples faced with the persecution and obviously intensifying over those first several chapters of the book of Acts and never really letting up as you continue through the book of Acts and you look at the journeys and the missionary trips and all that take place and the persecution and the difficulties, but yet those disciples would have to remember, and lo, I am with thee. The reality is that when we understand tonight that our shepherd is with us, it should bring us a measure of comfort.
